title: Of the Mortification of Sin in Believers

sections.heading: The Doctrine of Mortification
sections.html:
The Text and Its Claim

John Owen grounds his entire treatise on a single apostolic command:

Romans 8:13 — "For if ye live after the flesh, ye shall die: but if ye through the Spirit do mortify the deeds of the body, ye shall live."
Owen insists that every word of this text bears doctrinal weight. The promise of life is conditional upon mortification; the agent of mortification is the Holy Spirit, not the believer acting in naked self-will; and the object of mortification is "the deeds of the body" — indwelling sin expressing itself through the members. To miss any of these three poles is to misunderstand the entire enterprise.

The word "mortify" (Greek thanatoute) means to put to death, to deprive of power, to cause to wither. Owen is careful to distinguish this from suppression or mere outward restraint. A sin suppressed by fear of consequences, or crowded out by a competing worldly passion, has not been mortified. It remains alive and will reassert itself under favorable conditions. True mortification strikes at the root of the lust, weakening its indwelling power so that it no longer habitually dominates the believer's affections and choices.

What Mortification Is Not

Owen catalogs several counterfeits that pass for mortification in religious practice. First, the diversion of sin into a less scandalous channel is not mortification. A proud man who channels his pride into religious ambition has not mortified pride; he has given it a respectable dress. Second, occasional victories over a particular lust are not mortification. A man may resist a temptation in a moment of spiritual alertness and yet return to habitual compliance a week later. Third, self-imposed bodily austerities — fasting, vigils, physical penances — are not mortification in Owen's sense. Paul condemns these directly:

Colossians 2:23 — "Which things have indeed a shew of wisdom in will worship, and humility, and neglecting of the body; not in any honour to the satisfying of the flesh."
The flesh cannot mortify the flesh. Only the Spirit can do that work.

Fourth — and most important for Owen — a temporary amendment of life produced by conviction, sickness, or loss is not mortification. Many men become serious about sin when death is near, only to return to their former ways upon recovery. The cause of their restraint was not the indwelling Spirit but external pressure. Owen observes that Pharaoh repented repeatedly under plague and reversed himself as soon as each plague lifted (Exodus 9:27–35). That is the pattern of flesh-management, not Spirit-wrought mortification.

The Spirit as the Sole Agent

The hinge of Owen's argument is the prepositional phrase "through the Spirit." Mortification is a work of the Holy Spirit in the believer, not a work the believer performs by gathering sufficient moral resolve. This does not make the believer passive. Owen is emphatic that "the Holy Ghost works in us and with us, not against us or without us." The believer must set his mind on the things of the Spirit, must watch, must use the means of grace. But the power that actually weakens and eventually subdues a reigning lust is divine. Paul states the same principle elsewhere:

Philippians 2:13 — "For it is God which worketh in you both to will and to do of his good pleasure."

Owen draws a further consequence: only believers — those who have received the Spirit — can truly mortify sin. An unregenerate person may reform his outward behavior, but he has no spiritual principle capable of striking at the root of corruption. Mortification presupposes regeneration, and the exhortation of Romans 8:13 is addressed explicitly to those "in Christ Jesus" (Romans 8:1). To press this duty upon the unconverted as though they could perform it is to mislead them about both the nature of the gospel and the nature of sin.

sections.heading: The Nature and Means of Mortification
sections.html:
Knowing and Grieving the Sin

Owen prescribes a disciplined self-examination as the beginning of mortification. The believer must know the particular sin he is dealing with — its nature, its occasions, its disguises. General sorrow over sinfulness in the abstract accomplishes little. The physician who treats a disease must first identify it. Owen calls this getting a "clear and thorough sight" of the lust: its strength, how long it has operated, what fruit it has borne in the life, how it has injured God and neighbor. This examination is meant to produce a genuine, weighted grief — not neurotic self-accusation, but the sorrow of 2 Corinthians 7:10: "godly sorrow worketh repentance to salvation."

The grief must be felt in relation to God, not merely in relation to consequences. A man may regret a sin because it has damaged his reputation or his health without any genuine sorrow that he has offended a holy God. Owen distinguishes these clearly. The Psalmist's confession moves in the right direction:

Psalm 51:4 — "Against thee, thee only, have I sinned, and done this evil in thy sight."
David had wronged Bathsheba and murdered Uriah, yet he sees the sin first in its vertical dimension. That orientation is essential to genuine mortification.

Longing for Deliverance and Going to Christ

Owen insists that the mortified heart must be marked by a habitual longing for freedom from the corrupting power of the sin, not merely a desire to avoid its consequences or its shame. This longing is itself a Spirit-worked grace. Paul models it in Romans 7, where his anguished cry —

Romans 7:24 — "O wretched man that I am! who shall deliver me from the body of this death?"
— is immediately answered by the gospel: "I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord." The longing and the answer belong together. A believer who has resigned himself to a particular sin, who has stopped crying out about it, has already begun to lose the battle.

The decisive movement, however, is going to Christ by faith with the particular sin in view. Here Owen distinguishes his method sharply from both Roman penance and Stoic self-mastery. The believer does not bring himself to a state of worthiness before approaching Christ. He comes with the sin itself — its ugliness, its strength, its history — and pleads the sufficiency of Christ's blood and the intercession of the risen High Priest.

Hebrews 4:15–16 — "For we have not an high priest which cannot be touched with the feeling of our infirmities; but was in all points tempted like as we are, yet without sin. Let us therefore come boldly unto the throne of grace."
To mortify sin by any path that bypasses this explicit recourse to Christ is, in Owen's judgment, to deny the gospel in practice even while professing it in doctrine.

The Danger of Unmortified Sin

Owen devotes substantial attention to the progressive danger of neglected mortification. An unmortified lust does not remain static. It hardens the heart by repeated indulgence, dulls the conscience, weakens prayer, drains the joy from public worship, and creates a mounting debt of guilt that produces either despair or a false peace that is worse than despair. Owen traces this spiral in the life of David: his unconfessed sin after the adultery with Bathsheba darkened the next year of his life, dried up his psalms, and left him unable to deal faithfully with those around him (see Psalm 32:3–4).

Further, an unmortified lust opens the door to worse sins. Owen cites the principle that sin in the heart, when it cannot find its accustomed outlet, will seek another. The man who has given himself to covetousness will find that covetousness breeds fraud, then cruelty, then the abandonment of principle across a widening range of life. James traces this trajectory:

James 1:15 — "Then when lust hath conceived, it bringeth forth sin: and sin, when it is finished, bringeth forth death."
The only safe answer to this spiral is the immediate, violent, Spirit-empowered mortification that Owen urges from the text — "do mortify" being a present imperative, a continuous and ongoing action.

sections.heading: The Mortified Life and Christ
sections.html:
Union with Christ as the Root

In his closing argument Owen insists that the power of mortification flows from union with Christ — the believer's legal and vital solidarity with Christ in his death and resurrection. Paul makes this the theological foundation of all ethical exhortation in Romans 6:

Romans 6:6 — "Knowing this, that our old man is crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin."
The indicative precedes the imperative. The old man has been crucified — this is a done fact, reckoned to every believer by virtue of their union with Christ's death. Mortification is not the achievement of this union; it is the ongoing, experiential outworking of a union already accomplished.

Owen is careful not to collapse the indicative into the imperative or vice versa. The fact that the old man is crucified does not make mortification unnecessary; if anything, it makes it imperative, because the believer now has both the obligation and the resources to pursue it. The resurrection side of union provides the positive power: the same Spirit who raised Christ from the dead now dwells in the believer (Romans 8:11) and is the active agent in every act of genuine mortification.

The Gospel as the Power of Mortification

Against all forms of legal mortification — attempting to weaken sin by contemplating its punishment, stoking fear of hell, or multiplying religious duties — Owen argues that the gospel itself, received by faith, is the primary power of mortification. Beholding the love of God in the cross, the infinite condescension of Christ, the free and full pardon of every sin — this is what most powerfully breaks the dominion of lust over the believing heart. Paul argues in Romans 6:14 that "sin shall not have dominion over you: for ye are not under the law, but under grace." Paradoxically, the person most deeply grounded in free justification is most powerfully equipped to mortify sin, because the motivations of gratitude, love, and delight in God — which only the gospel can produce — are more powerful motivators than fear.

Owen returns here to the cross itself as a contemplative resource. He urges the believer to set before his mind, when the lust is pressing, the specific sufferings of Christ that are the remedy for that particular sin. Pride is mortified by contemplating the humiliation of the Son of God; lust by contemplating the purity and self-giving love of Christ; covetousness by contemplating Christ's voluntary poverty:

2 Corinthians 8:9 — "For ye know the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ, that, though he was rich, yet for your sakes he became poor, that ye through his poverty might be rich."
The cross, meditated upon specifically and repeatedly, is a mortifying force that no law can replicate.

Assurance and the Mortified Believer

Owen closes by addressing the relationship between mortification and the believer's assurance of salvation. He rejects two errors. The first is the error of those who tie assurance to the completeness of their mortification, as though they must be nearly free from indwelling sin before they can be confident of their standing before God. This is to make sanctification the ground of justification, which is a confusion of the order of grace. Assurance rests on Christ's finished work received by faith, not on the believer's progress in holiness.

The second error is the presumptuous confidence of those who claim assurance while making no serious effort at mortification. Owen is severe on this point: a man who habitually lives in known, unresisted sin has no warrant to claim the comfort of Romans 8:1 ("no condemnation") while refusing the obligation of Romans 8:13 ("do mortify"). The two verses belong to the same chapter and the same argument. Nevertheless, Owen ends with comfort. The very desire to mortify sin, the grief over its presence, the longing to be free — these are evidences of the Spirit's work and grounds for confidence. The believer who groans under indwelling sin as Paul groans in Romans 7:24 is not under condemnation; he is on the path of life, and the same Spirit who convicts will also deliver.
